content.title: Continuity at a Point
content.content:
A function $f: A \to \mathbb{R}$ is **continuous** 
at $c \in A$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta > 0$ 
such that
\[
|x - c| < \delta \text{ and } x \in A \implies |f(x) - f(c)| < \varepsilon.
\]
Equivalently (when $c$ is a limit point of $A$): $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)$.

content.title: Continuous Function
content.content:
A function $f: A \to \mathbb{R}$ is **continuous on $A$** if it is 
continuous at every point of $A$.

content.title: Sequential Characterization of Continuity
content.content:
$f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)$ in the 
domain with $x_n \to c$, we have $f(x_n) \to f(c)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Topological Characterization
content.content:
$f: A \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous if and only if for every open set 
$O \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, the preimage $f^{-1}(O) = \{x \in A : f(x) \in O\}$ 
is open in $A$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Algebra of Continuous Functions
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are continuous at $c$, then so are $f + g$, $fg$, $kf$ 
(for any constant $k$), and $f/g$ (provided $g(c) \neq 0$).
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Composition of Continuous Functions
content.content:
If $f$ is continuous at $c$ and $g$ is continuous at $f(c)$, then 
$g \circ f$ is continuous at $c$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Extreme Value Theorem
content.content:
If $f: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $f$ attains its maximum 
and minimum: there exist $x_M, x_m \in [a, b]$ such that 
$f(x_m) \leq f(x) \leq f(x_M)$ for all $x \in [a, b]$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
By Theorem [thm:heine-borel], $[a, b]$ is compact. We show $f([a, b])$ 
is compact (closed and bounded).

**Bounded:** If $f([a,b])$ were unbounded, there would exist $(x_n)$ 
in $[a,b]$ with $|f(x_n)| \to \infty$. By Bolzano-Weierstrass, $(x_n)$ has 
a convergent subsequence $x_{n_k} \to c \in [a,b]$. By continuity, 
$f(x_{n_k}) \to f(c)$, contradicting $|f(x_n)| \to \infty$.

**Closed:** Let $y_n \in f([a,b])$ with $y_n \to y$. Write $y_n = f(x_n)$ 
for $x_n \in [a,b]$. A subsequence $x_{n_k} \to c \in [a,b]$, and 
$f(x_{n_k}) \to f(c)$. Since $f(x_{n_k}) = y_{n_k} \to y$, we have $y = f(c) \in f([a,b])$.

Since $f([a,b])$ is closed and bounded, $\sup f([a,b])$ and $\inf f([a,b])$ 
are attained.

content.title: Intermediate Value Theorem
content.content:
If $f: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $L$ is between $f(a)$ and 
$f(b)$, then there exists $c \in (a, b)$ with $f(c) = L$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Assume $f(a) < L < f(b)$ (the other case is similar). Let 
$S = \{x \in [a,b] : f(x) < L\}$. Then $a \in S$ (so $S \neq \emptyset$) 
and $S$ is bounded above by $b$.

Let $c = \sup S$. We show $f(c) = L$.

**Claim: $f(c) \geq L$.** If $f(c) < L$, by continuity there exists 
$\delta > 0$ such that $f(x) < L$ for $x \in (c - \delta, c + \delta) \cap [a,b]$. 
This contradicts $c = \sup S$ since elements of $S$ would exist past $c$.

**Claim: $f(c) \leq L$.** If $f(c) > L$, by continuity there exists 
$\delta > 0$ such that $f(x) > L$ for $x \in (c - \delta, c + \delta) \cap [a,b]$. 
Then $c - \delta$ would be an upper bound for $S$, contradicting $c = \sup S$.

Thus $f(c) = L$.
